speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today is December the 10, 1996. My name is Linda Swimer, and I'm interviewing Albert Tausinger in London, England. And the language will be English. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Linda Swimer, and I'm interviewing Albert Tausinger on the 10th of December, 1996. If you can give me your name, Albert, and spell it for me-- your full name first.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Albert Tausinger. Yes? My parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spell?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes

speaker: interviewee
text: T-A-U-S-I-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am nearly 85.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born, Albert?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in-- now it called is [? Lodno-- ?] in Transylvania. It was the Austro-Hungarian monarchy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I am the youngest from seven. I have two-- four brothers and two sisters. Now I am the last one alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now can you tell me a little bit about your parents? What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father works in timber-- the timber industry. So he ran around all the Austro-Hungarian monarchy. It happens that from the seven children after 1918, the war-- end of the first war, we are born in five different countries. Now, my parents never have been abroad-- always in Austro-Hungarian monarchy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why-- why were you in five different countries? Can you explain that to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1918, the Austro-Hungarian monarchy split, and it was five independent countries. It was the Czechoslovakia, Austria, Hungarian, Rumania, Poland, and Prussia. So we are all born around-- around-- only in Austro-Hungarian monarchy. My parents never ever was abroad. They never had a passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every time your father got a job, your mother and all you children went together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went together. Went together up-- up to 1914. In 1914, when broke out the war, we evacuated back inland to Hungary-- to the north Hungary. After [? 18th, ?] it was Czechoslovakia. We brought [? up ?] back except two brothers. The one brother was in-- Walter was in the army, in the First World War. And in 1918, we have been in Czechoslovak. The border was Czechoslovakia. Now, origin, my father-- what I heard. I don't have documents. They come from Spain. They are Sephardins. My mother, she comes from Galicia. She was a ultra Orthodox Jewish-- a kohanim. So in the family about the religion was a little bit difficult. The mother was strong religious. The father and the boys wasn't. I had my bar mitzvah [? though ?] I wasn't religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how your parents met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: How your parents got to know each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: They met when my father works in Galicia by the timber. Some people-- he met there my mother. What happens? He married this. And when they have been under chuppah, the Orthodox Jewish, they want the mother cut the hair, to take a sheitel. So my father said, the hair are very nice. I don't like to cut it. When you cut the hair, I go out from the chuppah. So my mother had the hair still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did her father object to your father because he wasn't ultra Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At home, in the house, was strong kosher. Now, outside the house we eat everything. [? Now, ?] at home was strong kosher. Strong Friday evening, Erev Shabbos. Strong Yom Kippur, strong New Year, Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your home on a Friday night on Shabbos. What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal kosher meal. We eat. Nobody say the brokhe. Nobody know it. So it was difficult to say. Religious, [? pro ?] [? forma. ?] Strong religion at home between-- in the house. We are going in the synagogue twice a year plus in my bar mitzvah. Nobody goes to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in Central Europe, Austro-Hungarian, where we lived was only two Jewish groups. It was the Neolog group-- we belonged to them-- or-- and the Orthodox. [? No, ?] there was not a Orthodox like here-- ultra Orthodox. They were Orthodox. They had a small shul, and there was a Neolog synagogue. And did your mother go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother goes to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every week, your mother went to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember every week or not. No. She-- she was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home was, again, difficult. My mother spoke only German from home. She spoke Yiddish too-- German and Yiddish. So we travel around around. So my mother understand Hungarian. My older brother and sister, they go to the-- in Hungarian school and spoke Hungarian. Now, the mother-- when the brother, sister, talk to the mother in Hungarian, the mother always answer in German. So the-- my oldest brother, sister, they understand the German. They don't speak German. German speak only I and my sister, a younger sister. In 1918, I started the school one month in Hungarian. It was in September 1918. In October 1918, it was Czechoslovakia, independent Czechoslovakia. So I started again in the Slovak language. So my mother, again, the same trouble. She understood our Slovak. [? Now ?] always we talk in German. So actually, I was growing up in German language. So my German is very well. It is-- my English is very bad. I am sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at all. Not at all. And your father-- what did your father speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, he spoke Hungarian. He spoke German and Slovak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother and father spoke German together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me a nice memory of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: What bitte?

speaker: interviewer
text: Something you remember about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, what I remember him, he was always trouble with his leg. Before the First World War, in the-- on the woods, he fall in the winter in water. And [? since then, ?] he had frozen. And [? since then, ?] he had always trouble with his leg. So he goes only with a stick. I don't remember him to go normal without a stick. I don't remember my parents-- parents-- there are all only grey hair. The mother and the father of what I remember, only grey-haired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a strict father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Strict-- was he-- did he make many rules in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your mother. Can you tell me about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told you. The mother was-- comes from an ultra Orthodox Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you a nice memory of your mother, something special?

speaker: interviewee
text: Special-- I was the youngest from seven. So I was a young boy. So I-- my mother give me everything. The oldest brother, sister, they was [? out ?] from house away. And the first what I have-- not a trouble with my mother or the mother with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When my sister, she was married and have a boy, a child-- [? the ?] [? Hermonie. ?] In this time, I was 10 years old. And the nephew was born. So what happens? It was the first grandchild. And my mother always look about the grandchild while my sister was working. So I-- I was afraid I don't like my grandson-- my nephew. You know? Now after, it was all right. After it started the other nephews and nieces. Tell me a bit about your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers and sisters-- the oldest brother was lucky. He was on the front in 1918, somewhere in Italy. He got a holiday and come home in 1918 [INAUDIBLE]. My second brother, he was a little bit [? worst. ?] He was in the army and was a prisoner of war in Russia. He married there a Russian Orthodox girl and come back in 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the first time in 1922 that the family was all together. I don't remember-- I lost absolutely-- when married my oldest brother. I don't remember when married my older sister. I lost the time. Did you get on well with your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I get very well. [? No, ?] it was the trouble. I was a 10-year-old youngster, and my brother was nearly 20. It was a different-- nearly a generation. So they have grown up people, and I was a child always. So it-- contact between my brother and-- and the older sister was difficult. The age different. The sister was 14 years older. When I was 10 years old, my sister was 24 years old. She had a child. I was still a child. Close I was and near to my second sister. [? He ?] was here in Britain, in London. She died here. So with her, we were close. It was only four years age different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Katiza, ?] Katerina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell me a bit about school.

speaker: interviewee
text: School?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? No, ?] I-- I started-- I started in 1918 when I was six years old in the Austro-Hungarian monarchy-- started the school at six years. So I started in September. It was still Austro-Hungarian monarchy in a Hungarian school. In 1918, it was independent Czechoslovakia. So I started in a Slovak school. It was so in Zilina where we live. There was three elementary schools. It was a state school, it was a Catholic school, and it was a Jewish school. Well, I started in the Jewish school, in Slovak in German. It was interesting. In the Jewish school in Zilina, was nearly the half the children were German. They don't go in the state school. They don't go in the Catholic school. They go to the Jewish school where they was teaching German too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mean the children were German Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Wasn't Jewish. German. Origin German they were in Zilina. There were a lot of-- I don't know how say English-- volksdeutsche.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: German who lives not in Germany. He lives around Europe, groups. So there was children from the so-called volksdeutsche. They go to the Jewish school. And relation, actually, I must tell you, between the German-- the volksdeutsche-- and the Jewish in Zilina was very nice, very good. And they prefer to be friend with the Jewish while they can talk always German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your best friend in school Jewish, or not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. I had a lot Jewish. And I have friends German too. We go always-- I like skiing. So we go out for skiing. You can put on the skis on the front of the house. There was started to snow November, December, up to April. And we go on the mountains. And the second my sport was [? canoistic. ?] And there were a lot-- some Slovaks. [? No, ?] mostly German-speaking people. Jewish and German and few Slovak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your house to me where you lived, in Zilina?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1918, when we run away from the-- where my father worked in the timber-- to Zilina, it was outside. It was-- we lived in a flat without electricity, 1918. It was a two-room flat, and we had been-- I don't remember-- five, six people. So in one room we sleep four, five children, the parent.

speaker: interviewer
text: After, I don't remember the-- the year. We found a house. It was-- yes. In this where we live was no toilet, no running water. We-- we have the flat on the first floor. And the toilet, you must run down to the garden. No. I don't remember. It was in 1920, '21? It was a widower-- a widow. She moved to Vienna, and we rent from her the house. So it was started-- we started to live normally with running water, with toilet. And there was three or four rooms. And this house was just near Zilina?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was in Zilina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. In Zilina.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zilina.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you run away to Zilina?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was where my father works in the timber. And local people, they don't like the Jewish. So we run in the town. [? That ?] wasn't-- no exist for my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- was your father hurt by the non-Jews outside Zilina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. Again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father-- you said the non-Jewish people outside Zilina didn't like your father because he was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only my father. They don't like the Jewish, the Slovak. This was a small village--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they do anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the timber factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hurt him, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. They don't hurt. They take everything from us. They come and take everything what you find in the-- in the house. It was the-- it wasn't the anti-Semitism. It [? was ?] the things what you take away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you there? You saw them take everything away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared? Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old I was? Five and a half year old. Six-- not six. It was in 1918 when the war finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come in the day or at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Mostly day. All I remember. It's 80 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever experience any anti-Semitism in Zilina, when you moved to Zilina? As a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: As child in Zilina, I don't feel anti-Semitism. Zilina was a town-- how you call it-- a free Austro-Hungarian town. It was a small town. It was 17,000, 18,000 inhabitants. There was Jewish, Hungarian-- so-called-- to be exact, it was so-called Hungarian Slovak. They were Slovak. [INAUDIBLE] all speak Hungarian. And volksdeutsche, the German. So, these three groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these three groups, they hold nice together. It wasn't anti-Semitism. Anti-Semitism I started, to be honest, after 1918. What happens? The Slovaks, the so-called Slovaks, they don't have Slovak intelligence. The intelligence-- in this time in, the [? Slovakie ?] was Hungarian-speaking. [? There ?] was nationality Slovaks, you know? [? No, ?] speaks Hungarian, not Slovak.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the '20s, the Czechs-- the other part of Czechoslovakia-- they sent the intelligent-- intelligence to [? Slovakie. ?] And the business-- the shops and this-- was in the hand mostly 90% in Jewish. The Jewish had the-- the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Czechs sent teachers and workers and everything. Now, to take the shops, they started the anti-Semitisms. In the [? Slovakie, ?] especially in Zilina, the anti-Semitism started with the Czechs, not with the Slovaks. What do you remember about anti-Semitism in Zilina as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Am ?] a child?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. When you were walking along, did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- to-- only the anti-Semitism was the Czech youngsters what are the childrens who around my age. They hold more together. They don't like the-- the German don't like the Jewish. They contact mostly with the-- with the Slovak, the Hungarian Slovaks. Not the German and not the Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you belong to any youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] a child-- I was in the Maccabi. It was a gymnastic and skiing. It was a group Maccabi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go with your sister, or just by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself. My sister-- when I was 10, she was 14. When I started with the 12, 14 years, she was a 20 years. And she married, I think, after [INAUDIBLE] 17. She married-- she was 18 years old when she married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me one of your favorite memories? A special-- something special that you remember from your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my childhood, special was only what I liked and was very nice was the skiing and the [? canoistic. ?] I was a fanatic skier and [? canoeist ?] and tourist. We walk around the mountains. Zilina was-- is in the mountains. Same the town was, I think, 600 meter over the sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go with friends, or by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Mostly with friends. Always with friends. No by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother bake any special food for you that you liked? Did she cook anything special that you liked? [LAUGH] You don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: We like everything in this time. We was always hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there always enough food to eat in the house? Was there enough food in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, always. We eat normally in the morning coffee and bread. And lunch and evening. And actually it was between morning and lunch have always some coffee or bread with butter. And after the lunch, 4 o'clock, again, we had a coffee or something. No. It was not this coffee what we drink here. It was-- I don't know how it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after-- after your first school, where-- you went to a senior high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my first school, I go-- I finish the-- it was-- in Austro-Hungarian monarchy, was the first school, from 6 to 10. Then was the middle school from 10 to 14, 15. After my-- when I was 15, I go to the business academy. It was a business school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll just stop there, and we'll change the tape. And we'll carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
